14. Information Geometry

We’ve viewed inference in data (raw ) or feature (sufficient statistic) space as partitioning . Now we’ll look at distribution space.

We developed KL divergence as a meaningful way to measure how different two distributions are with respect to the task of inference: is the cost you get for using distribution instead of in with log cost.

Remarks

  • Continue with discrete, finite alphabet. Countably and uncountably infinite alphabets require extra care.
  • Use sometimes
  • Define for continuity
  • with equality iff .

  • Prop. is convex in as well as :

14.1. Euclidean Geometry

A convex set satisfies .

Define projection onto set as the nearest point in that set. If you have a convex set , the angle where is any point in , is a point outside the set, and is ‘s projection is at least . If is a hyperplane, then . Pythagoras for convex sets then says

(14.2)

with equality if and only if .

Suppose we’re in and want to know how many points project onto in -dimensional hyperplane . This is the orthogonal complement of (shifted so with is the center) and is -dimensional

Today we will show that we get similar results using

Denote as the collection of distributions over . When is finite, this is a probability simplex. For example corresponds to the plane in the first octant.

Examples

  • The weighted geometric and arithmetic mean form a curve and a line respectively on the simplex.
  • The expectation constraint is a linear constraint and yields an dimensional hyperplane (an existing constraint is the simplex constraint).

14.2. Projections of Distributions

Definition 14.1 (I-Projection). The information projection of a distribution onto a (nonempty and closed) set of distributions is the distribution such that

(14.3)

Remarks

  • Because is not symmetric, there’s two different notions of projection. [TODO] We will get to the other one later. There’s a beautiful duality.
  • is not necessarily unique. When the set of distributions is convex, then it is unique, as it becomes a convex optimization probleem as is convex in (proved in L14 appendix).

(14.4)

acts like a distance squared!

Pythagoras corollary: The -projection of any not on the boundary of the simplex cannot lie on the boundary of the simplex unless all of is confined to that particular boundary component (intuition: need to get finite D on the RHS. LHS is finite since is an interior point.).

14.3. Linear Families

Definition 14.2 (Linear Family). A set of distributions is a linear family it can be written as the set of distributions in a simplex satisfying expectation (linearity) constraints:

Say the representation of a linear family is minimal if the matrix has linearly independent rows.

The dimension of the original simplex is . By rank-nullity, . Beautifully, we can’t get redundancy with the simplex constraint since it would yield a row of zeros.

Proposition 14.1. is a convex set. More generally it applies for any such that .
Proof

Obvious by lineraity of expectation.

□

Proposition 14.2 (Pythagorean Identity).

TLDR: The general pythag requires slope to be nonpositive in the direction of the projected point. Linear families you interpolate and extrapoatle negative so this forces the slope to be zero.

14.4. Orthogonal Families

Given a linear family with a minimal representation corresponding to a particular choice of . Then for any , there is a unique choice of such that is in this linear family: .

What is the set of all distributions in the simplex whose -projection onto is .

Theorem 14.2 (Orthogonality of Linear and Exponential Families).

This theorem gives a procedure to find the I-projection of any distribution onto a linear family specified by and that is a nonlinear system of equations. Parameterize as exponential (invert the equation in the theorem statement) and apply the constraint.
